news_summary: Recent advances reveal groundbreaking progress in aging and muscle regeneration, emphasize the importance of mental health research visibility, and uncover transformative insights into Japanese ancestry through DNA discoveries.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées récentes révèlent des progrès révolutionnaires dans les domaines du vieillissement et de la régénération musculaire, soulignent l'importance de la visibilité de la recherche en santé mentale et apportent des éclairages novateurs sur les origines japonaises grâce à des découvertes en matière d'ADN.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances revelan avances revolucionarios en el campo del envejecimiento y la regeneración muscular, destacan la importancia de dar visibilidad a la investigación sobre salud mental y aportan conocimientos transformadores sobre los orígenes japoneses a través de descubrimientos relacionados con el ADN.

articles.title: A grad student’s wild idea sparks a major aging breakthrough
articles.summary: A casual conversation between graduate students helped spark a breakthrough in aging research at Mayo Clinic. Researchers discovered that tiny synthetic DNA molecules called aptamers can selectively attach to senescent “zombie cells,” which are linked to aging, cancer, and neurodegenerative disease. The method could eventually help scientists identify and target these cells in living tissue with far greater precision.
articles.content:
One of the biggest challenges in aging and disease research is tracking down senescent cells. These cells -- often called "zombie cells" -- stop dividing but refuse to die off normally. Over time, they can build up in the body and have been linked to conditions including cancer, Alzheimer's disease, and the aging process itself.

Scientists have been exploring ways to remove or repair these harmful cells, but there has been a major obstacle. Researchers have struggled to reliably identify senescent cells hiding among healthy cells in living tissue.

DNA Aptamers Help Researchers Identify Senescent Cells

A team at Mayo Clinic now says it has found a promising new strategy. Writing in the journal Aging Cell, the researchers describe a technique that uses molecules called "aptamers" to tag senescent cells.

Aptamers are short strands of synthetic DNA that naturally fold into complex three dimensional shapes. Those shapes allow them to attach to specific proteins found on the surfaces of cells.

Working with mouse cells, the scientists screened more than 100 trillion random DNA sequences and identified several rare aptamers capable of binding to proteins associated with senescent cells. Once attached, the aptamers effectively flagged the cells for identification.

"This approach established the principle that aptamers are a technology that can be used to distinguish senescent cells from healthy ones," says biochemist and molecular biologist Jim Maher, III, Ph.D., a principal investigator of the study. "Though this study is a first step, the results suggest the approach could eventually apply to human cells."

A Chance Conversation Sparked the Discovery

The project began with an unexpected idea shared during a casual conversation between graduate students at Mayo Clinic.

Keenan Pearson, Ph.D. -- who recently earned his degree from Mayo Clinic Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences -- had been studying how aptamers might be used against brain cancer or neurodegenerative diseases while working with Dr. Maher.

Elsewhere on campus, Sarah Jachim, Ph.D., -- who was also completing graduate research at the time -- was studying aging and senescent cells in the laboratory of Nathan LeBrasseur, Ph.D.

The two students crossed paths during a scientific event and started discussing their thesis projects. Dr. Pearson began wondering whether aptamer technology could be adapted to recognize senescent cells.

"I thought the idea was a good one, but I didn't know about the process of preparing senescent cells to test them, and that was Sarah's expertise," says Dr. Pearson, who became lead author of the publication.

Researchers Pursue a "Crazy" Idea

The students presented the idea to their mentors as well as researcher Darren Baker, Ph.D., whose work focuses on therapies targeting senescent cells.

Dr. Maher says the concept initially sounded "crazy," but intriguing enough to investigate further. The mentors ultimately embraced the collaboration.

"We frankly loved that it was the students' idea and a real synergy of two research areas," says Dr. Maher.

The research advanced quickly. Early experiments produced encouraging findings sooner than expected, leading the team to bring in additional students from several labs.

Then-graduate students Brandon Wilbanks, Ph.D., Luis Prieto, Ph.D., and M.D.-Ph.D. student Caroline Doherty contributed specialized techniques, including advanced microscopy and analysis of a wider variety of tissue samples.

"It became encouraging to expend more effort," Dr. Jachim says, "because we could tell it was a project that was going to succeed."

New Clues About the Biology of Zombie Cells

The study may offer more than just a new way to identify senescent cells. It also uncovered information about the cells themselves.

"To date, there aren't universal markers that characterize senescent cells," says Dr. Maher. "Our study was set up to be open-ended about the target surface molecules on senescent cells. The beauty of this approach is that we let the aptamers choose the molecules to bind to."

Several of the aptamers attached to a variation of fibronectin, a protein found on the surface of mouse cells. Researchers do not yet understand exactly how this fibronectin variant relates to senescence, but the finding could help scientists better define what makes senescent cells unique.

Future Potential for Aging and Disease Treatments

The researchers caution that additional studies will be needed before aptamers can reliably identify senescent cells in humans.

Still, the technology could eventually become much more than a detection tool. Scientists believe aptamers might one day carry therapies directly to senescent cells, allowing highly targeted treatment approaches.

Dr. Pearson says aptamers are also less expensive and more adaptable than traditional antibodies, which are commonly used to distinguish different types of cells.

"This project demonstrated a novel concept," says Dr. Maher. "Future studies may extend the approach to applications related to senescent cells in human disease."
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific breakthrough in aging research with the discovery of aptamers that can selectively identify senescent cells linked to aging and diseases such as cancer and Alzheimer's. This advancement has broad implications for health and disease treatment, offering a promising new method to detect and potentially target harmful cells with precision. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful real-world impact on public health.
articles.personality_title: New DNA tool helps scientists spot harmful aging cells
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long struggled to find and study senescent cells, often called “zombie cells.” These cells stop dividing but don’t die like normal cells, and they can cause problems like aging, cancer, and brain diseases.

**What happened** – Researchers at Mayo Clinic discovered that tiny synthetic DNA strands called aptamers can stick to these senescent cells. This idea came from a casual chat between two graduate students working on different projects. They tested over 100 trillion DNA sequences on mouse cells and found some aptamers that attach to proteins unique to senescent cells, making these harmful cells easier to identify.

**Impact** – This is the first time aptamers have been shown to reliably mark senescent cells, which was very hard before. Knowing exactly which cells are senescent helps scientists study aging and diseases more closely. The study also found new clues about the proteins on these cells, which could lead to better treatments.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to test this method on human cells and explore if aptamers can deliver medicines directly to senescent cells. Since aptamers are cheaper and more flexible than current tools, this could improve future therapies for aging-related diseases.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new DNA-based method using aptamers can identify harmful aging cells, opening doors for better research and targeted treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouvel outil ADN aide les scientifiques à repérer les cellules nocives du vieillissement
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques ont longtemps eu du mal à trouver et étudier les cellules sénescentes, souvent appelées « cellules zombies ». Ces cellules arrêtent de se diviser mais ne meurent pas normalement, ce qui peut causer le vieillissement, le cancer et des maladies du cerveau.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de la Mayo Clinic ont découvert que de petits brins d’ADN synthétiques appelés aptamères peuvent se fixer sur ces cellules sénescentes. Cette idée est née d’une conversation entre deux étudiants diplômés travaillant sur des projets différents. Ils ont testé plus de 100 000 milliards de séquences d’ADN sur des cellules de souris et ont trouvé des aptamères qui se fixent à des protéines uniques aux cellules sénescentes, facilitant ainsi leur identification.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que des aptamères permettent de marquer de façon fiable les cellules sénescentes, ce qui était très difficile auparavant. Identifier précisément ces cellules aide les scientifiques à mieux étudier le vieillissement et les maladies. L’étude a aussi révélé de nouveaux indices sur les protéines présentes sur ces cellules, ce qui pourrait aider à développer de meilleurs traitements.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent tester cette méthode sur des cellules humaines et voir si les aptamères peuvent aussi transporter des médicaments directement vers les cellules sénescentes. Comme les aptamères sont moins chers et plus flexibles que les outils actuels, cela pourrait améliorer les thérapies futures contre les maladies liées au vieillissement.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle méthode à base d’ADN utilisant des aptamères peut identifier les cellules nocives du vieillissement, ouvrant la voie à de meilleures recherches et traitements ciblés.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva herramienta de ADN ayuda a científicos a detectar células dañinas del envejecimiento
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han tenido dificultades para encontrar y estudiar las células senescentes, llamadas a menudo “células zombis”. Estas células dejan de dividirse pero no mueren normalmente, lo que puede causar envejecimiento, cáncer y enfermedades cerebrales.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Clínica Mayo descubrieron que pequeños fragmentos de ADN sintético llamados aptámeros pueden adherirse a estas células senescentes. Esta idea surgió de una conversación casual entre dos estudiantes de posgrado que trabajaban en proyectos diferentes. Probaron más de 100 billones de secuencias de ADN en células de ratón y encontraron aptámeros que se unen a proteínas únicas en las células senescentes, facilitando su identificación.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que los aptámeros pueden marcar confiablemente las células senescentes, algo que antes era muy difícil. Identificar con precisión estas células ayuda a los científicos a estudiar mejor el envejecimiento y las enfermedades. El estudio también descubrió nuevas pistas sobre las proteínas en estas células, lo que podría ayudar a crear mejores tratamientos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren probar este método en células humanas y explorar si los aptámeros pueden entregar medicinas directamente a las células senescentes. Como los aptámeros son más baratos y flexibles que las herramientas actuales, esto podría mejorar las terapias futuras para enfermedades relacionadas con el envejecimiento.

**Frase clave** – Un nuevo método basado en ADN usando aptámeros puede identificar células dañinas del envejecimiento, abriendo puertas para mejor investigación y tratamientos dirigidos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of intertwined glowing DNA strands shaped like delicate keys gently unlocking and illuminating dormant, cracked cell-like spheres labeled as "zombie cells," set against a soft, natural background of subtle molecular patterns and abstract laboratory glassware shapes, symbolizing the breakthrough in identifying and targeting senescent cells with aptamer technology.

articles.title: Rebooting stem cells builds aged muscles and assists injury recovery
articles.summary: Muscle stem cells, which are crucial for building new muscle, don’t work as well as we get older, but giving them an artificial boost could rejuvenate them
articles.content:
Muscle stem cells, which are crucial for building new muscle, don’t work as well as we get older, but giving them an artificial boost could rejuvenate them

A scanning electron micrograph of an embryonic muscle cell differentiating in to a skeletal muscle cell STEVE GSCHMEISSNER/SCIENCE PHOTO LIBRARY

Old mice grow bigger muscles and recover from injuries better when stem cells are taken out of their aged muscles, given a reboot, then put back in. A similar approach may allow rejuvenation of ageing muscles in people too.

“In theory, if you took an elderly person’s muscle stem cells out, charged them up and put them back in, they would probably be more functional,” says James White at Duke University in North Carolina.

Muscle stem cells generally lie dormant in muscle tissue, but when a muscle is injured, they grow into new muscle cells to patch up the damage. “Muscles rely very much on regeneration because they’re so susceptible to mechanical strain,” says White. “When you work out and feel sore, that’s muscle damage. The immune system talks to the stem cells and they repair the damage by making new muscle from scratch.”

With age, however, muscle stem cells decline in number and become worse at regenerating injured tissue. In experiments in mice, White and his colleagues found that this is because aged muscle stem cells contain less of an enzyme called glutaminase. This affects their ability to produce fatty molecules like palmitate and oleate, known as lipids, which are fundamental building blocks of cells. “Stem cells have to get multiple times larger to turn into muscle cells, so they need lipids to build cell membranes and also to use for energy,” says White.

To correct this deficiency, the researchers took muscle stem cells from old mice and supplemented them with extra palmitate and oleate in a dish. These replenished stem cells were then injected into injured leg muscles of other old mice. Over the next few days, the mice grew new muscle fibres in their injured legs that were 45 per cent bigger than when they received untreated muscle stem cells. The treatment also resulted in better leg function when the mice walked on a treadmill and did other mobility tests.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

In people, levels of glutaminase enzyme also decline with age in muscle stem cells, which may explain why our muscles become smaller and weaker as we get older and don’t bounce back as well from injury. These muscle changes often reduce mobility and quality of life, while also increasing the risk of falls.

We may be able to reduce or reverse this muscle deterioration by boosting levels of glutaminase enzyme or palmitate and oleate lipids in muscle stem cells, says team member David Lee, also at Duke University. “We’re now looking for ways in which we can translate this to clinical approaches,” he says.

Swallowing glutaminase, palmitate or oleate in the form of oral supplements probably wouldn’t work because not enough would reach the tiny stem cells in muscles, says White. There would also be a risk of driving cancer, since cancer cells have similar ways of growing that use the same ingredients, he says. However, removing stem cells from older people’s muscles, activating them with enzymes or nutrients in a lab and then putting them back in may be safer and more effective, he says.

Young bodybuilders or athletes would not be able to gain bigger muscles or enhance recovery this way because they do not have stem-cell deficiencies to begin with, says White. “Their muscles are already chock full of stem cells and they’re all very functional and ready to go,” he says.

In related research, Florida-based company Longeveron is investigating whether infusions of young people’s stem cells might rejuvenate muscles and reduce frailty in older people. The company harvests mesenchymal stem cells, which have the capacity to turn into various cell types including muscle cells, from the bone marrow of healthy donors aged 18 to 45. When frail volunteers aged 75 to 80 received intravenous infusions of these young stem cells in a clinical trial, they were able to walk further afterwards.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in rejuvenating aged muscle stem cells to improve muscle growth and injury recovery, which has broad implications for aging populations. It provides detailed experimental evidence in mice and discusses potential clinical applications for humans, addressing a major health challenge related to aging and mobility.
articles.personality_title: Boosting aged muscle stem cells helps muscles grow and heal better
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – As people get older, their muscles become weaker and do not heal as quickly from injuries. This happens because muscle stem cells, which help repair and build muscle, lose their ability to work well with age.

**What happened** – Scientists at Duke University studied old mice and found that their muscle stem cells had less of an important enzyme called glutaminase. This enzyme helps the cells make fats that are needed to grow new muscle. The researchers took muscle stem cells from old mice, gave them extra fats in the lab, and then put them back into injured muscles of other old mice. The treated mice grew muscles that were 45% bigger and moved better than mice that got untreated cells.

**Impact** – This discovery shows a way to improve muscle repair in aged animals by boosting the stem cells’ ability to build muscle. Since humans also lose glutaminase in muscle stem cells as they age, this method could help older people recover from injuries and keep muscles stronger. Stronger muscles can improve mobility and reduce the risk of falls.

**What's next step** – The research team is exploring how to safely apply this treatment to humans. Instead of taking enzyme or fat supplements by mouth, which may not reach muscle stem cells well and could be risky, they plan to remove stem cells from a person’s muscles, recharge them in the lab, and then put them back. Other groups are also testing stem cell infusions from young donors to help older adults improve muscle strength.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists found a way to recharge aged muscle stem cells to help muscles grow bigger and heal faster, offering hope for better recovery in older people.
articles.personality_title_fr: Stimuler les cellules souches musculaires âgées améliore la croissance et la guérison des muscles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En vieillissant, nos muscles deviennent plus faibles et guérissent plus lentement après une blessure. Cela s’explique par le fait que les cellules souches musculaires, qui réparent et construisent le muscle, fonctionnent moins bien avec l’âge.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’université Duke ont étudié des souris âgées et ont découvert que leurs cellules souches musculaires contenaient moins d’une enzyme importante appelée glutaminase. Cette enzyme aide les cellules à fabriquer des graisses nécessaires pour produire du nouveau muscle. Les chercheurs ont prélevé des cellules souches musculaires de souris âgées, leur ont ajouté des graisses en laboratoire, puis les ont réinjectées dans les muscles blessés d’autres souris âgées. Les muscles des souris traitées étaient 45 % plus gros et elles se déplaçaient mieux que celles ayant reçu des cellules non traitées.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre qu’il est possible d’améliorer la réparation musculaire chez les animaux âgés en renforçant les capacités des cellules souches. Comme les humains perdent aussi de la glutaminase dans leurs cellules souches musculaires avec l’âge, cette méthode pourrait aider les personnes âgées à mieux récupérer de leurs blessures et à garder des muscles plus forts. Des muscles plus forts améliorent la mobilité et réduisent les risques de chutes.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe de recherche cherche des moyens sûrs d’appliquer ce traitement aux humains. Au lieu de prendre des suppléments par la bouche, qui pourraient ne pas atteindre les cellules souches musculaires et comporter des risques, ils envisagent de retirer les cellules souches des muscles d’une personne, de les recharger en laboratoire, puis de les réintroduire. D’autres groupes testent aussi des infusions de cellules souches jeunes pour aider les personnes âgées à renforcer leurs muscles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont trouvé un moyen de recharger les cellules souches musculaires âgées pour aider les muscles à grandir et à guérir plus vite, offrant une nouvelle piste pour améliorer la récupération chez les personnes âgées.
articles.personality_title_es: Estimular las células madre musculares envejecidas mejora el crecimiento y la recuperación muscular
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – A medida que las personas envejecen, sus músculos se debilitan y tardan más en sanar tras una lesión. Esto ocurre porque las células madre musculares, que ayudan a reparar y construir músculo, funcionan peor con la edad.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad de Duke estudiaron ratones viejos y encontraron que sus células madre musculares tenían menos de una enzima llamada glutaminasa. Esta enzima ayuda a las células a fabricar grasas necesarias para crear nuevo músculo. Los investigadores sacaron células madre musculares de ratones viejos, les añadieron grasas en el laboratorio y luego las inyectaron en músculos lesionados de otros ratones viejos. Los ratones tratados desarrollaron músculos un 45 % más grandes y caminaron mejor que los que recibieron células sin tratar.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra una forma de mejorar la reparación muscular en animales envejecidos al aumentar la capacidad de las células madre para crear músculo. Como en los humanos también disminuye la glutaminasa en estas células con la edad, este método podría ayudar a las personas mayores a recuperarse mejor de las lesiones y mantener músculos más fuertes. Músculos más fuertes mejoran la movilidad y reducen el riesgo de caídas.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo de investigación está buscando maneras seguras de aplicar este tratamiento en humanos. En lugar de tomar suplementos orales, que podrían no llegar bien a las células madre musculares y ser riesgosos, planean extraer las células madre de los músculos de una persona, activarlas en el laboratorio y luego reintroducirlas. Otros grupos también están probando infusiones de células madre jóvenes para ayudar a los adultos mayores a mejorar su fuerza muscular.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos hallaron cómo recargar las células madre musculares envejecidas para que los músculos crezcan más y sanen mejor, ofreciendo una opción para mejorar la recuperación en personas mayores.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a pair of aged, gently glowing muscle fibers intertwined with vibrant, seed-like stem cells being nurtured and energized by flowing streams of golden lipids and enzymes, symbolizing rejuvenation and healing within the muscle tissue.

articles.title: Mental-health research is too often invisible — it is time to change that
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 14 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01550-4Mental illness needs visibility more urgently than almost any other area of medicine and health care. A new award from Wellcome and Nature aims to raise its prominence.
articles.content:
Mental illness needs visibility more urgently than almost any other area of medicine and health care. A new award from Wellcome and Nature aims to raise its prominence.

The outcomes of mental-health research do not always reach those who could benefit.Credit: Anastasiia Sienotova/Getty

More than one billion people worldwide — around one person in seven — are estimated to live with a mental-health condition, according to the World Health Organization. Anxiety and depression alone affect close to 700 million, and each year around 700,000 individuals take their own lives, with more than half of those doing so before the age of 50.

You would think that the scale of the problem would merit a proportionate investment. But overall, such a response is lacking, meaning that many people with mental illness struggle to access the treatment, care or support that they need. Governments spend a median of only 2% of total health budgets on mental-health services — and much less in low- and middle-income countries. Severe shortages of trained mental-health professionals persist almost everywhere.

The picture for research funding is more mixed. After a period of sustained growth between 2014 and 2020, overall funding for mental-health research globally entered a steep decline, and by 2023 had fallen back to 2014 levels in real terms. That’s according to an analysis published last week by the International Alliance of Mental Health Research Funders in Washington DC (see go.nature.com/4wqefbt).

Postdoc depression and anxiety rates are rising, finds survey of 872 researchers

Some 80% of all such funding comes from the United States, where the budget for the National Institute of Mental Health is under severe threat. By contrast, in the European Union, the United Kingdom and Australia, investment has been rising significantly. For instance, the global biomedical funder Wellcome in London spent £138 million (US$186 million) on mental health in 2025, according to its annual report (see go.nature.com/4v4xe55).

However, there is a common consensus that the research being done is not reaching those whose lives could be improved by it. One reason is a lack of awareness outside the field. Today sees the launch of an initiative intended to make a tangible, if modest, difference.

Wellcome, in partnership with Nature, is announcing a prize for mental-health research that we hope will boost recognition of the field and its potential to change the lives of people with mental illness. The Wellcome Prize for Mental Health Science with Nature will award US$1 million to an overall winner for developing innovative strategies, treatments or support mechanisms that deliver measurable improvements in mental-health outcomes. Three further finalists will receive $250,000 each. The winner will be announced next June.

The prize, the largest of its kind globally, will highlight mental-health interventions that are rigorous, evidence-based and at an early stage of research. Eligible submissions, which can be from anywhere in the world, will need to be supported by peer-reviewed research and demonstrate measurable benefit alongside the potential for wider adoption.

Updates to the ‘bible’ for mental-health conditions will miss the mark — is it time to ditch the DSM?

‘Intervention’ is used deliberately broadly. A submission could involve a drug, digital platform, school-based programme, workplace initiative, policy reform or community-led model for mental-health services. The emphasis will be less on the category and more on outcome and impact: the winning intervention must demonstrate that the lives of people with mental-health conditions will improve.

This prize comes at a point of great creativity and ambition in mental-health research. Among treatment methods being investigated by researchers are non-invasive brain stimulation, psychedelic-assisted psychotherapy, software-based treatments (known as digital therapeutics) and real-time behavioural monitoring. These are taking place not only in hospitals and laboratories, but also in schools, workplaces and communities.

Submissions will be judged by an international panel drawn from academia, industry and policy, which includes editors of Nature, Nature Medicine and Nature Mental Health. All panel members are recognized for their expertise. Some have direct experience of mental-health challenges, too — something that is viewed as crucial to ensuring that research is of high quality and that psychological support is both sensitive and effective (Nature Rev. Psychol. 4, 143; 2025).

The award is but one step towards correcting an historical injustice. As an accompanying Editorial in Nature Mental Health says: “For much of modern history, mental health interventions were defined less by therapeutic intent or impact than by social control.” (Nature Mental Health 4, 689–690; 2026). People with serious mental illness were often regarded as dangerous and morally or mentally deficient, creating stigma and fear. They were treated as not fully human, leading to abhorrent medical practices from those who were meant to be caring for them.

A single prize will not by itself tackle the broader problem of mental-health research failing to reach the people it is intended to help. But prizes draw attention and create public narratives around progress in fields that might otherwise remain fragmented or invisible outside specialist circles. A prize of this scale therefore functions as more than recognition. It will make an argument that will reach those who need to hear it: that mental-health research is a domain of serious scientific discovery that produces medical advances of consequence.

We hope that all those who have responsibility, health-care policymakers especially, will use the knowledge gained through the prize to improve the lives of people living with mental-health conditions. That cannot be too much to ask.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant positive development in mental health research by announcing a major global prize aimed at increasing visibility, funding, and impact of mental health interventions. This initiative addresses a critical global health issue affecting over a billion people and aims to foster innovative, evidence-based solutions with measurable benefits. The prize's scale, international scope, and focus on improving lives provide meaningful and broad societal impact.
articles.personality_title: New global prize aims to boost mental health research and care
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
More than one billion people worldwide live with mental health conditions like anxiety and depression. Despite this, mental health care and research often receive little funding and attention compared to other health areas. Many people cannot get the help they need, especially in poorer countries.

**What happened**
Wellcome, a global biomedical funder, and the journal Nature have launched a new prize to highlight important mental health research. The Wellcome Prize for Mental Health Science with Nature will award $1 million to one winner and $250,000 to three finalists. The prize will recognize research projects that show clear, positive results in improving mental health. It is open to researchers worldwide, and winners will be announced in June 2027.

**Impact**
This prize is the largest of its kind and focuses on research that can make real differences in people’s lives. It encourages new ideas like digital therapy, brain stimulation, and community support programs. By shining a spotlight on mental health research, the prize hopes to increase awareness and funding, helping more people get better care.

**What's next step**
Researchers from around the world will submit their projects for the prize. An expert panel will review the entries to find the most promising work. The prize can inspire other funders and governments to invest more in mental health research and services.

**One-sentence takeaway**
A new $1 million global prize aims to raise awareness and support for mental health research that improves lives worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau prix mondial pour stimuler la recherche et les soins en santé mentale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
Plus d'un milliard de personnes dans le monde vivent avec des troubles de santé mentale comme l'anxiété et la dépression. Pourtant, les soins et la recherche en santé mentale reçoivent souvent peu de financement et d'attention par rapport à d'autres domaines de la santé. Beaucoup ne peuvent pas obtenir l'aide dont ils ont besoin, surtout dans les pays pauvres.

**Ce qui s'est passé**
Wellcome, un organisme mondial de financement biomédical, et la revue Nature ont lancé un nouveau prix pour mettre en lumière la recherche importante en santé mentale. Le Prix Wellcome pour la science de la santé mentale avec Nature attribuera 1 million de dollars à un gagnant et 250 000 dollars à trois finalistes. Le prix récompensera des projets de recherche montrant des résultats clairs et positifs pour améliorer la santé mentale. Il est ouvert aux chercheurs du monde entier, et les gagnants seront annoncés en juin 2027.

**Impact**
Ce prix est le plus important de ce genre et met l'accent sur des recherches qui peuvent réellement changer la vie des gens. Il encourage de nouvelles idées comme la thérapie numérique, la stimulation cérébrale et les programmes de soutien communautaire. En attirant l'attention sur la recherche en santé mentale, le prix espère augmenter la sensibilisation et le financement, aidant ainsi plus de personnes à recevoir de meilleurs soins.

**Prochaine étape**
Les chercheurs du monde entier soumettront leurs projets pour le prix. Un jury d'experts examinera les candidatures pour trouver les travaux les plus prometteurs. Le prix peut encourager d'autres bailleurs de fonds et gouvernements à investir davantage dans la recherche et les services en santé mentale.

**Message clé en une phrase**
Un nouveau prix mondial de 1 million de dollars vise à augmenter la visibilité et le soutien à la recherche en santé mentale qui améliore la vie partout dans le monde.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo premio mundial para impulsar la investigación y atención en salud mental
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
Más de mil millones de personas en el mundo viven con problemas de salud mental como la ansiedad y la depresión. Sin embargo, la atención y la investigación en salud mental reciben poca financiación y atención en comparación con otras áreas de la salud. Muchas personas no pueden obtener la ayuda que necesitan, especialmente en países pobres.

**Qué pasó**
Wellcome, una organización global de financiación biomédica, y la revista Nature lanzaron un nuevo premio para destacar la investigación importante en salud mental. El Premio Wellcome para la Ciencia de la Salud Mental con Nature otorgará 1 millón de dólares a un ganador y 250,000 dólares a tres finalistas. El premio reconocerá proyectos de investigación que muestren resultados claros y positivos para mejorar la salud mental. Está abierto a investigadores de todo el mundo, y los ganadores se anunciarán en junio de 2027.

**Impacto**
Este premio es el más grande de su tipo y se enfoca en investigaciones que pueden mejorar realmente la vida de las personas. Fomenta ideas nuevas como terapias digitales, estimulación cerebral y programas de apoyo comunitario. Al dar visibilidad a la investigación en salud mental, el premio busca aumentar la conciencia y la financiación, ayudando a que más personas reciban mejor atención.

**Próximo paso**
Investigadores de todo el mundo enviarán sus proyectos para el premio. Un panel de expertos revisará las propuestas para elegir los trabajos más prometedores. El premio puede inspirar a otros financiadores y gobiernos a invertir más en investigación y servicios de salud mental.

**Resumen en una frase**
Un nuevo premio mundial de 1 millón de dólares busca aumentar la visibilidad y el apoyo a la investigación en salud mental que mejora vidas en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, open book radiating light onto a diverse group of interconnected, stylized brain-shaped lanterns floating gently above a calm, abstract landscape, symbolizing the illumination and sharing of mental-health research to brighten and support minds worldwide.

articles.title: Who are the Japanese? Huge DNA discovery rewrites history
articles.summary: Scientists analyzing the genomes of thousands of people across Japan discovered evidence for a previously overlooked third ancestral group, challenging the long-accepted “dual origins” theory. The newly identified ancestry appears linked to the ancient Emishi people of northeastern Japan. Researchers also uncovered inherited Neanderthal and Denisovan DNA connected to conditions like diabetes, heart disease, and cancer.
articles.content:
For decades, scientists believed the Japanese population largely descended from two ancient groups: the Jomon hunter-gatherers who lived in the archipelago for thousands of years, and later migrants from East Asia who brought rice farming and new technologies to Japan.

But a major genetic analysis from researchers at RIKEN's Center for Integrative Medical Sciences suggests the picture is far more complicated.

Using whole-genome sequencing on more than 3,200 people from across Japan, the team found evidence supporting a third ancestral group tied to northeastern Asia and possibly linked to the ancient Emishi people. The findings, published in Science Advances, add powerful support to the increasingly discussed "tripartite origins" theory of Japanese ancestry.

The results also revealed something else surprising: Japan's population is genetically more diverse than many researchers once assumed.

"The Japanese population isn't as genetically homogenous as everyone thinks," said Chikashi Terao, who led the study at RIKEN. "Our analysis revealed Japan's subpopulation structure on a fine scale, which is very beautifully classified according to geographical locations in the country."

A Massive DNA Map of Japan

To investigate Japan's deep genetic history, researchers analyzed DNA samples collected from seven regions stretching from Hokkaido in the north to Okinawa in the south. The project became one of the largest whole-genome studies ever conducted on a non-European population.

Instead of relying on older DNA microarray methods, the team used whole-genome sequencing, which reads nearly all three billion DNA base pairs in a person's genome. According to the researchers, this provides roughly 3,000 times more information than traditional techniques.

"Whole-genome sequencing gives us the chance to look at more data, which helps us find more interesting things," Terao explained.

The scientists then combined the genetic information with medical histories, disease diagnoses, family histories, and clinical test results to build a large database known as the Japanese Encyclopedia of Whole-Genome/Exome Sequencing Library (JEWEL).

One especially important focus involved rare genetic variants. These uncommon DNA changes can sometimes preserve clues about ancient migration patterns and long-lost ancestral populations.

"We reasoned that rare variants can sometimes be traced back to specific ancestral populations, and could be informative in revealing fine-scale migration patterns within Japan," Terao said.

The Hidden Third Ancestor

The analysis uncovered striking regional differences across Japan.

Jomon ancestry appeared strongest in Okinawa, where it was found in 28.5% of samples, while western Japan showed much lower levels at 13.4%. Researchers found that people in western Japan had stronger genetic connections to Han Chinese populations, likely reflecting major migration waves from continental East Asia between 250 and 794 CE. Those migrations also coincided with the spread of Chinese-style government systems, writing, and education throughout Japan.

The newly identified Emishi-related ancestry was concentrated in northeastern Japan and became less common farther west.

The findings build on earlier ancient DNA studies published in 2021 that first proposed the idea that modern Japanese people descend from three major ancestral sources instead of two. Those studies suggested that a third migration connected to the Kofun period played a major role in shaping modern Japan.

Recent follow-up studies have continued strengthening that idea. Researchers analyzing ancient genomes and skeletal remains have found increasing evidence that multiple migration waves entered Japan over centuries, creating a much more layered population history than previously believed.

Ancient Neanderthal and Denisovan DNA Still Affects People Today

The study also explored genetic material inherited from Neanderthals and Denisovans, two ancient human groups that interbred with Homo sapiens tens of thousands of years ago.

Scientists have become increasingly interested in why some of these ancient DNA fragments survived in modern humans while others disappeared. In many cases, the inherited genes appear linked to health, adaptation, or disease risk.

For example, earlier studies showed that Tibetans inherited a Denisovan-related version of the EPAS1 gene that may have helped humans survive in high-altitude environments. Researchers also previously identified Neanderthal-derived DNA associated with severe Covid-19 complications in some populations.

The Japanese genome study identified 44 archaic DNA regions still present in modern Japanese populations, many of them unique to East Asians. One Denisovan-derived region inside the NKX6-1 gene was associated with type 2 diabetes and may influence how some patients respond to semaglutide treatments.

Researchers also found 11 Neanderthal-derived genetic segments connected to conditions including coronary artery disease, prostate cancer, and rheumatoid arthritis.

Toward Personalized Medicine

Beyond tracing ancestry, the researchers believe the work could eventually improve healthcare.

The team identified potentially harmful variants in the PTPRD gene that may be linked to hypertension, kidney failure, and myocardial infarction. They also found common loss-of-function variants in the GJB2 and ABCC2 genes, which are associated with hearing loss and chronic liver disease.

"What we've tried to do is to find and catalog loss-of-function gene variants that are very specific to Japanese people, and to understand why they are more likely to have some specific traits and diseases," Terao said. "We'd like to connect population differences with differences in genetics."

The study reflects a broader shift happening in genetics research. For years, most large genomic databases heavily focused on people of European ancestry, limiting scientists' understanding of disease risk in other populations.

Terao hopes expanding JEWEL with more Asian genomic data will help change that.

"It's quite important to expand this to the Asian population so that in the long run, the results can benefit us too," he said.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a major scientific breakthrough in understanding Japanese ancestry through large-scale whole-genome sequencing, revealing a previously unknown third ancestral group and uncovering genetic variants linked to health conditions. This discovery has broad significance for population genetics and personalized medicine, potentially improving healthcare outcomes for millions of people in Japan and beyond. The study also addresses the underrepresentation of Asian populations in genomic research, which is a meaningful advancement for global health equity.
articles.personality_title: New DNA study reveals a third ancestor in Japanese history
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For a long time, scientists thought the Japanese people came from two main ancient groups: the Jomon, who lived in Japan for thousands of years, and later migrants from East Asia who brought farming and new tools.

**What happened** – Researchers at RIKEN studied the complete DNA of over 3,200 Japanese people from all across the country. They found evidence of a third ancestral group connected to the ancient Emishi people of northeastern Japan. This discovery challenges the old idea of just two ancestors. The study also found that Japanese people carry DNA from Neanderthals and Denisovans, ancient human relatives, which is linked to health conditions like diabetes and heart disease.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows the Japanese population is more genetically diverse than we thought. Finding a third ancestor changes how we understand Japan’s history and migration. Also, knowing about inherited ancient DNA helps scientists learn why some people have certain health risks. This could lead to better treatments tailored to Japanese patients.

**What’s next step** – The researchers plan to keep adding more DNA data from Asian populations to the JEWEL database. This will help improve genetic research and healthcare for Japanese people and others in Asia. They also want to study how these genetic differences affect diseases, moving toward personalized medicine.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A large DNA study uncovered a previously unknown third ancestor in Japan and linked ancient genes to health, opening new paths for understanding history and improving medicine.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle étude ADN révèle un troisième ancêtre dans l’histoire japonaise
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant longtemps, les scientifiques ont pensé que le peuple japonais descendait de deux groupes anciens : les Jomon, qui ont vécu au Japon pendant des milliers d’années, et des migrants venus d’Asie de l’Est apportant l’agriculture et de nouveaux outils.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs du RIKEN ont étudié l’ADN complet de plus de 3 200 Japonais venant de tout le pays. Ils ont découvert un troisième groupe ancestral lié aux anciens Emishi du nord-est du Japon. Cette découverte remet en question l’idée ancienne de seulement deux ancêtres. L’étude a aussi trouvé que les Japonais portent de l’ADN de Néandertaliens et Denisoviens, des humains anciens, lié à des maladies comme le diabète et les maladies cardiaques.

**Impact** – Cela montre que la population japonaise est plus diverse génétiquement que ce qu’on croyait. Trouver un troisième ancêtre change notre compréhension de l’histoire et des migrations du Japon. Connaître cet ADN ancien aide aussi à comprendre les risques de santé, pour mieux soigner les patients japonais.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent ajouter encore plus de données ADN d’Asie à la base JEWEL. Cela améliorera la recherche génétique et la santé au Japon et en Asie. Ils souhaitent aussi étudier comment ces différences génétiques influencent les maladies, vers une médecine plus personnalisée.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une grande étude ADN a révélé un troisième ancêtre inconnu au Japon et lié des gènes anciens à la santé, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour comprendre l’histoire et améliorer la médecine.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo estudio de ADN revela un tercer ancestro en la historia japonesa
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los científicos creyeron que el pueblo japonés descendía de dos grupos antiguos: los Jomon, que vivieron en Japón por miles de años, y migrantes posteriores de Asia Oriental que trajeron la agricultura y nuevas tecnologías.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de RIKEN estudiaron el ADN completo de más de 3,200 japoneses de todo el país. Encontraron evidencia de un tercer grupo ancestral vinculado a los antiguos Emishi del noreste de Japón. Este hallazgo desafía la idea antigua de solo dos ancestros. También descubrieron que los japoneses tienen ADN heredado de neandertales y denisovanos, humanos antiguos, relacionado con enfermedades como diabetes y problemas cardíacos.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que la población japonesa es más diversa genéticamente de lo que se pensaba. Encontrar un tercer ancestro cambia la forma en que entendemos la historia y las migraciones en Japón. Además, conocer este ADN antiguo ayuda a entender por qué algunas personas tienen ciertos riesgos de salud, lo que puede mejorar tratamientos médicos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean seguir añadiendo más datos de ADN de poblaciones asiáticas a la base de datos JEWEL. Esto ayudará a mejorar la investigación genética y la salud para los japoneses y otros en Asia. También quieren estudiar cómo estas diferencias genéticas afectan las enfermedades, avanzando hacia la medicina personalizada.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un gran estudio de ADN descubrió un tercer ancestro desconocido en Japón y relacionó genes antiguos con la salud, abriendo nuevos caminos para entender la historia y mejorar la medicina.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, warm-toned map of Japan composed of overlapping translucent DNA double helix strands, each colored subtly to represent three distinct ancestral groups—Jomon, East Asian migrants, and Emishi—flowing seamlessly across the archipelago from north to south, with delicate patterns inspired by ancient pottery and traditional textiles subtly woven into the background to symbolize cultural heritage and genetic diversity.
